method3.generated_article:
==Name==
The name “Brecqhou” refers specifically to the small island lying west of Sark, while “Sark” may refer both to the main island and to the wider jurisdiction that includes Brecqhou and nearby islets and rocks.[1] The island is also recorded with the variant spelling “Brechou”.[5] In the medieval period, after Jeanne de Chesney married Denis Le Marchant of Guernsey, ownership passed through the Le Marchant family and the island became known as the “Isle des Marchants”.[2] That name continued to be used even after ownership of the island later passed to other families.[2]

==Geography==
Brecqhou is one of the Channel Islands in the English Channel off the Normandy coast, within the Bailiwick of Guernsey, a British Crown dependency.[2][3] Geographically, it is closely associated with Sark, which lies about 11 km east of Guernsey and about 40 km west of the Cherbourg Peninsula.[4] The island is separated from Great Sark by the narrow Le Gouliot Channel.[4]

The Channel Islands list Brecqhou among their inhabited islands, although it is described as a private island with only a tiny population compared with Sark.[1][2] Geologically, Brecqhou belongs to the Bailiwick of Guernsey group that contains the oldest rocks in the Channel Islands.[5] Most of Sark and Brecqhou is formed of metamorphic rocks, principally schists and gneisses, whose structures record several phases of early Proterozoic deformation and metamorphism around 2,020 million years ago.[5]

==Fort Brecqhou==
Fort Brecqhou is a private castellated residence built for Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay after they acquired the tenement of Brecqhou in 1993 for £2.3 million.[2] Designed by the British architect John Quinlan Terry, construction began in 1994 and was completed in 1996.[2] The house is a granite-clad castle on a quadrangular plan, built in the castellated Gothic style with circular turrets at its four corners.[2] Its design includes large twisted chimneys derived from an English architectural style developed during the reign of Henry VIII.[2] The project, including the house, gardens and outbuildings, was estimated to have cost about £90 million.[2] It has been described as the largest house built in Britain for at least two centuries.[1][2] The building was constructed using two types of Spanish granite and has been characterized as a fortress-like country house occupying a rocky crown.[1] Fort Brecqhou remains fully extant and in private ownership, with public access limited to the grounds by appointment.[2] Peter J. Rivett’s history of Brecqhou treats the Barclays’ purchase of the island and the construction of Fort Brecqhou as the concluding modern chapter in the island’s history.[3]

==Flag and stamps==
References to a Brecqhou flag appear mainly in popular or vexillological contexts, including online material describing a black-and-white flag associated with the island, but the available sources do not establish a formal official status for it.[1] Discussions of such symbols are shaped by the wider political sensitivity of Brecqhou’s relationship with Sark, which has been described in island-studies literature as involving jurisdictional contestation between the island’s owners and Sark’s authorities.[2][4]  

There is no evidence in the supplied sources of a separate Brecqhou postal administration or independent Brecqhou stamp issues.[5] Postal and philatelic matters relevant to the island are therefore best understood through the postal history of Guernsey, whose Bailiwick-wide system developed from an official post office established in 1794 and gained postal independence in 1969, enabling distinctive Guernsey stamp issues.[5]

==Public visits==
Brecqhou is privately owned and lies off the west coast of Sark, and access for visitors is not open to the general public.[1][4] A 2010 exterior lighting management plan for Sark stated that, after the island was sold to the Barclay brothers in 1993, visits to Brecqhou were “by invitation only”.[4] The island is described as a residence of the Barclay family, with a small number of island employees, rather than as a publicly accessible visitor destination.[2][4]

==Notes and references==
Sources on Brecqhou often treat it within the legal and political context of Sark, and one study notes that references to Sark as a jurisdiction may include Brecqhou unless the two islands are expressly distinguished.[2]

The island’s separate symbolic identity has been discussed in relation to privately produced flags and carriage labels, including Leonard Matchan’s 1969 labels and later issues by subsequent owners.[2]

Academic discussion of modern Sark politics frequently refers to Brecqhou in connection with disputes involving Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay and debates over development, governance and Sark’s institutions.[3]

Cartographic coverage of Brecqhou is provided by locally produced Bailiwick of Guernsey mapping rather than by the Ordnance Survey of Britain.[4]

Natural-history and travel accounts mention Brecqhou mainly as a visible landmark from Sark and nearby waters, including observations of its cliffs, coastline and the mansion built there by the Barclay brothers.[5]

Linguistic and regional studies list Brecqhou among the smaller islands of the Channel Islands archipelago, providing wider context for its place within the island group.[1]

==Feudal relationship with Sark==
Sark’s modern feudal constitution is usually traced to Queen Elizabeth I’s 1565 grant of the Seignory to Helier de Carteret, who held it as a fief haubert and owed personal homage and military service to the monarch.[2]  
The grant required de Carteret to keep Sark free from the Queen’s enemies, and later accounts describe the island as divided into forty tenements with reciprocal duties of defence and tithe.[2][3]  
Brecqhou’s place within that feudal structure has long been disputed: one interpretation treats it as part of Sark’s fief because of its close defensive relationship with Sark, while Rivett argued that it was not originally part of the fief and was claimed only later.[1]  
The status of Brecqhou as a Sark tenement has also been challenged by writers who argue that its treatment as such depended on later reinterpretations of historical documents and customary law.[4]  
Legal advice given during a 1949 sale nevertheless described Brecqhou as a dependency of Sark, stated that Sark law applied there, and characterised its owner as a tenant in perpetuity holding a manorial freehold comparable to other Sark tenements.[4]  
Official descriptions now commonly place Brecqhou within the jurisdiction of Sark, with the Ministry of Justice describing the Bailiwick of Guernsey as comprising Guernsey, Alderney and Sark, the last of which includes Brecqhou.[1]  
Because Brecqhou is treated as a tenement of the fief of Sark, the legislative authority of Chief Pleas and the laws of Sark generally extend to the island.[1]  
At the same time, some Sark laws have been disapplied to Brecqhou, giving it a special status and a limited degree of autonomy compared with other tenements.[1]  
The relationship became politically contentious after Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay acquired Brecqhou in 1993 and challenged Sark’s feudal institutions, including the treizième, a payment of one-thirteenth of a property purchase price formerly payable to the Seigneur.[3]  
Their legal and political pressure contributed to reforms including the abolition of male-preference inheritance rules and, in 2007, replacement of the treizième with a property transfer tax payable to the island government.[3]  
Sark’s feudal system of government was replaced in 2008 by an elected assembly, after the Privy Council approved reforms introducing universal voting rights and a 28-member legislature.[1][5]
